text.fullText: February 17, 2026 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 RE: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 To Whom it May Concern: The Upper Mattaponi Tribe respectfully submits this request for an extension of the public comment period for the proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 regulatory requirements. The Federal Register notice provides only 30 days for public comment. Given the scope, complexity, and potential impacts of this rulemaking, that timeframe is not sufficient to allow for meaningful Tribal engagement, technical review, intergovernmental coordination, or government-to-government consultation. Section 401 of the Clean Water Act is one of the most important tools available to states and Tribes to protect water quality from federally licensed or permitted projects. Changes to its scope, interpretation, or implementation have far-reaching consequences for water resources, Tribal sovereignty, and treaty-reserved rights. A rule of this magnitude demands careful legal and scientific analysis. Tribal governments must also evaluate how the proposal affects cultural resources, subsistence practices, and long-term environmental stewardship responsibilities. Thirty days does not allow for this level of review. We therefore request a 180-day extension of the public comment period. At an absolute minimum, no less than 90 days should be provided. This additional time is necessary for Tribes to consult with leadership, coordinate with neighboring jurisdictions, engage technical experts, and fully assess the implications of the proposal. It is also necessary to meet the federal government’s trust responsibility and ensure meaningful consultation with federally recognized Tribal Nations. EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin has publicly stated that “EPA is committed to working government to government with federally recognized Tribes” and that Tribal engagement is central to EPA’s mission of protecting human health and the environment. A 30-day comment period for a rule of this magnitude is inconsistent with that commitment. Meaningful government-to-government engagement requires time, transparency, and respect for Tribal sovereignty. Importance of Section 401 and Cooperative Federalism Congress enacted the Clean Water Act with a clear and ambitious purpose to “restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.” The Act recognizes that states and Tribes play a critical role in achieving that purpose. Section 401 embodies this cooperative federalism framework by allowing states and authorized Tribes to review federal permits or licenses that may result in discharges to Waters of the United States and to ensure that those activities comply with water quality requirements. There is no credible evidence that the Section 401 certification process is fundamentally broken. States and Tribes across the country have used this authority responsibly for decades. In the Chesapeake Bay region, Section 401 certifications have served as an essential safeguard for one of the most ecologically and economically important estuaries in the United States. Where Tribes or states do not have authority, EPA serves as the certifying authority. This balanced system reflects the structure Congress intended. The Chesapeake Bay restoration effort provides a powerful lesson. Decades of pollution led to severe ecosystem decline, requiring sustained, coordinated federal, state, and Tribal action to begin recovery. That progress remains fragile. Weakening Section 401 authority or reducing review timelines would place that progress at risk. It is much easier to contaminate water than it is to restore it (e.g., the nearly 8,000 gallons of jet fuel that accidentally got released into the James River on February 13, 2026). Decisions made under Section 401 directly affect drinking water supplies, fisheries, wetlands, submerged aquatic vegetation, and habitat that communities across the Bay and United States. Timelines and Procedural Safeguards The current certification process appropriately balances efficiency and environmental protection. Most certifications are issued well within existing timelines. The full one-year period is typically necessary only for the most complex projects, where technical analysis and public input are essential. The 2023 Certification Improvement Rule provided additional clarity, including a six- month default timeline when parties cannot otherwise agree. Shortening state and Tribal review periods, or limiting public comment opportunities, would undermine sovereign authority and create unreasonable incentives. Applicants could submit incomplete or insufficient applications and allow the clock to run out, resulting in waivers of certification authority. This would shift power away from sovereign governments and toward project applicants. If procedural changes are considered, the burden should be placed on applicants to provide complete information within a defined timeframe. The timeline for state and Tribal review should not be shortened. Public comment periods should not be further restricted. Many Tribes operate environmental programs with limited staffing and funding. Compressing timelines disproportionately harms Tribal Nations and reduces their ability to assert sovereign authority. Water systems are complex, and the risks of contamination can be severe and long-lasting. A deliberate and thorough review process is not an obstacle to progress. It is a necessary safeguard for public health and environmental protection. Tribal Sovereignty and Treatment as a State For Tribal Nations, Section 401 authority is not simply an administrative process. It is a recognition of inherent sovereignty and a mechanism for protecting treaty-reserved rights and reserved water resources. Congress enacted Section 518(e) of the Clean Water Act to ensure that Tribes can be treated in a similar manner as states for purposes of administering water quality programs. This provision reflects the federal trust responsibility and affirms that Tribes have the authority and responsibility to protect waters within their jurisdiction. Repealing or limiting the regulatory recognition of standalone Treatment as a State authority for Sections 401 and 401(a)(2) would weaken Tribal self-governance in environmental decision making. Removing clear regulatory pathways that allow Tribes to exercise Section 401 authority on their own could create real barriers to participation. Consultation is important, but it is not the same as having regulatory authority. Without clear and accessible certification authority, Tribes could be left in advisory roles instead of having enforceable decision-making power over projects that directly affect their waters and resources. Linking Section 401 authority exclusively to Section 303 Treatment as a State status could create additional administrative barriers. Many Tribal environmental programs operate with limited staff and financial resources. Requiring additional approvals before exercising certification authority could delay participation in critical decisions or discourage Tribes from seeking authority altogether, Such barriers reduce fair access to federal environmental programs and conflict with long standing federal policy that supports Tribal self-determination. Water holds deep cultural, spiritual, and subsistence importance for Tribal Nations. It sustains fisheries, wildlife, culturally significant plants, and ceremonial practices that define community identity and continuity. Section 401 authority allows Tribes to protect these living systems for future generations. Rescinding that authority sends a troubling signal about the federal government’s commitment to Tribal sovereignty. Protection of Narrative Water Quality Standards Equally concerning is any suggestion that Section 401 certification decisions should rely solely on numeric water quality standards. Water quality standards are made up of four core parts: designated uses, numeric criteria, narrative criteria, and antidegradation policies. Each of these components plays an essential role in protecting water resources. Narrative criteria are not optional or secondary. They are federally approved, legally enforceable standards that exist because not all forms of water pollution can be measured by a single number. Many forms of water quality harm cannot be reduced to a specific pollutant concentration. Habitat destruction, streambank erosion, sediment buildup, biological impairment, nuisance algae growth, foul odors, and long-term ecosystem stress often result from a combination of factors. These impacts may develop gradually and may not immediately violate a numeric threshold. Narrative standards allow regulators to address these broader conditions by requiring that waters remain free from substances or activities that cause harm to aquatic life, wildlife, or human uses. Without narrative criteria, regulators would be forced to ignore serious ecological damage simply because it does not exceed a specific numeric value. This issue is especially serious for wetlands. Wetlands are among the most productive and valuable ecosystems in the country. They filter pollutants, reduce flooding, recharge groundwater, store carbon, and provide critical habitat for fish, birds, and other wildlife. In regions such as the Chesapeake Bay watershed, wetlands play a central role in improving overall water quality by trapping sediment and nutrients before they reach rivers and the Bay itself. Yet water quality standards for wetlands are frequently written in narrative form rather than numeric. Wetland health is often measured by vegetation condition, hydrology, soil characteristics, and biological integrity rather than a single pollutant concentration. If certification decisions were limited to numeric standards only, wetlands could effectively be excluded from meaningful protection under Section 401. This would create a major regulatory gap. Activities that drain, fill, fragment, or degrade wetlands might escape full review if they do not violate a specific numeric pollutant limit. Such an outcome would undermine decades of scientific understanding about the ecological importance of wetlands. Excluding or minimizing narrative standards artificially narrows the lawful scope of review and reduces the ability to protect interconnected water systems. For Tribal Nations, numeric-only limitations are particularly harmful. Tribal water quality standards often reflect culturally specific uses that depend on healthy ecosystems, including fisheries, wildlife habitat, culturally significant plants, and ceremonial waters. These uses require protection of biological integrity and overall ecosystem function, not just compliance with isolated pollutant numbers. Wetlands are often culturally significant landscapes that support traditional foods, medicines, and practices. A certification process that disregards narrative standards fails to account for these relationships and disregards Tribal knowledge and stewardship. A scientifically sound and legally defensible Section 401 certification process must recognize both numeric and narrative standards as equally enforceable. Water systems are complex and interconnected. Effective protection requires tools that reflect that complexity. Limiting review to numeric thresholds alone ignores ecological reality, weakens wetland protection, and undermines the Clean Water Act’s core goal of restoring and maintaining the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. Changes to “Neighboring Jurisdiction” The proposed change replacing the long-standing term “neighboring jurisdictions” with “other States” raises serious concerns. Section 401(a)(2) of the Clean Water Act was written to ensure that downstream and adjacent sovereign governments have a meaningful opportunity to review and object to federally permitted projects that may affect their water quality. EPA has stated that “other States” includes Tribes with Treatment as a State status. However, replacing clearer language with a narrower term creates confusion and may unintentionally limit Tribal participation. If Tribes are not explicitly and consistently recognized, they could face barriers in receiving notice, raising objections, or requesting conditions to protect their waters. Even if the intent is not to exclude Tribes, the revised wording may weaken the cooperative federalism structure of the Clean Water Act and conflict with EPA’s long-standing practice of treating eligible Tribes in a similar manner as states for water quality protection. Any change that affects how Tribes are recognized in this process should be clearly explained and carefully justified to avoid undermining Tribal sovereignty and downstream water protections. Conclusion The Clean Water Act’s Section 401 program is working as Congress intended. It reflects true cooperative federalism, safeguards water quality, and respects the sovereign authority of states and Tribal Nations. There is no credible evidence that the program is broken or that it requires weakening. Where implementation challenges exist, they should be addressed in ways that strengthen water protections, improve clarity, and support sound decision-making, not in ways that accelerate approvals at the expense of environmental integrity and long-term public interest. Weakening Section 401 authority or narrowing the scope of certification review would not only threaten water quality, but it would also impose serious and measurable economic harm. The Chesapeake Bay watershed supports an economy valued at more than $100 billion annually, including commercial and recreational fisheries, tourism, agriculture, and outdoor recreation. Recreational fishing alone contributes more than $1 billion each year in economic activity. When water quality declines, these sectors are immediately affected. Harmful algal blooms, sedimentation, and habitat degradation reduce fish populations, close shellfish beds, disrupt tourism, and strain local economies. Restoration is far more expensive than prevention. Since the 1980s, federal and state partners have invested over $20 billion in Chesapeake Bay restoration, with total long-term costs projected to reach tens of billions more. Weakening preventative safeguards now risk repeating past mistakes and compounding those costs. Degraded water quality increases drinking water treatment expenses, burdens aging infrastructure, and heightens public health risks. These costs fall on Tribal governments that often operate with limited budgets and staffing. A diminished Section 401 framework would shift financial responsibility away from project applicants and onto Tribes, states, and taxpayers, who would ultimately bear the long-term costs of ecosystem decline, lost fisheries, damaged cultural resources, and water treatment upgrades. Strong certification authority is not only environmentally sound policy, but also fiscally responsible governance. For the Chesapeake Bay, for Tribal Nations, and for future generations who depend on clean and reliable water, EPA must maintain the substantive framework of the 2023 Certification Improvement Rule, preserve standalone Treatment as a State authority, reject numeric-only limitations, retain existing procedural timelines, and ensure meaningful government-to- government consultation. Protecting water quality is not an obstacle to economic progress. It is the foundation of public health, ecological resilience, stable regional economies, and Tribal sovereignty. This proposed rule does not correct a failing system. Instead, it risks creating uncertainty, weakening proven safeguards, and exposing communities to long-term environmental and economic harm. The practical course is clear: strengthen what works, uphold sovereign authority, and protect the waters that sustain us all. Sincerely, Liz Buikema Environmental Protection Manager Upper Mattaponi Tribe King William, VA
